sections.text: Welcome to English One, Module Three — Sentence Structure and Syntax. Strong writing is not just good vocabulary. It is built on four sentence types and the right joining moves. Today you learn to identify and write simple, compound, complex, and compound-complex sentences, and to fix the two errors that quietly wreck student essays — fragments and run-ons.

Here is the trap. A student writes — I love the library, I go there every day. It feels natural. It is wrong. That comma is doing the work of a period, and it cannot. This is a run-on, the most common student error in graded writing. By the end of this lesson, you will see it instantly and have four clean ways to fix it.

Game plan. First, the building block — independent and dependent clauses. Second, the four sentence types and the punctuation each one needs. Third, the two errors — fragments and run-ons — and how to repair them. Then we pause twice — once to label sentence types, once to fix broken sentences. Same skills the assignment asks for.

Every sentence is built from clauses. An independent clause has a subject and a verb and expresses a complete thought — it can stand alone. A dependent clause also has a subject and a verb, but it begins with a subordinating word like because, when, or although. It cannot stand alone. Spot the clauses, and the sentence type becomes obvious.

Four sentence types. Simple is one independent clause. Compound is two independent clauses joined by a comma and a FANBOYS conjunction — for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so — or by a semicolon. Complex is one independent clause plus a dependent clause. Compound-complex combines them — two independent clauses plus at least one dependent clause.

The most common mistake — the comma splice. Students join two complete sentences with only a comma and think they have written a compound sentence. They have not. A compound sentence needs a comma plus a FANBOYS conjunction, or a semicolon. Just a comma between two complete thoughts is a run-on dressed up to look correct.

Trace it. Start with a simple sentence — Maya studied. Add a second independent clause with comma and a FANBOYS — Maya studied, and she passed the test. That is compound. Now add a dependent clause with because — Because she practiced daily, Maya studied, and she passed the test. That is compound-complex. Same idea, four different builds.

Your turn. Read these four sentences on screen. For each one, name the type — simple, compound, complex, or compound-complex. Look for the joining word and the comma — that is your evidence. Pause now. Label all four. Press play when you are ready.

Time. Sentence one — The dog barked loudly — is simple, one clause. Sentence two — She studied hard, but she still felt nervous — is compound, comma plus FANBOYS. Sentence three — Although it rained, we hiked the trail — is complex, dependent clause first. Sentence four — When the bell rang, the students cheered, and the teacher smiled — is compound-complex, one dependent plus two independent.

One more. On screen are two broken sentences — one fragment and one run-on. Rewrite each one as a correct sentence. For the run-on, use any of the four fixes — period, comma plus FANBOYS, semicolon, or subordinating word. Pause now. Fix both. Press play when ready.

Time. The fragment was — Because the test was hard. It has a dependent clause but no independent clause. Fix — Because the test was hard, I studied for hours. The run-on was — I love pizza I eat it every day. Four clean fixes — period between them, comma plus and, semicolon, or — Because I love pizza, I eat it every day. Any of those is correct.

Now the assignment payoff. You will write five examples of each sentence type — twenty original sentences, on topics you choose. Then you will revise ten provided fragments and run-ons into correct sentences, and explain what made each original wrong. Vary your structure. Real writers mix all four types — that is what makes paragraphs sound rhythmic instead of choppy.

Recap. Independent clause stands alone. Dependent clause cannot. Four sentence types — simple, compound, complex, compound-complex. Compound joins with comma plus FANBOYS or a semicolon. Comma alone equals a run-on. Fix run-ons four ways — period, comma plus FANBOYS, semicolon, or subordinator. Mix the types when you write.

How to actually master this. Read the Purdue OWL page on sentence structure for clear examples of each type. Run the Khan Academy practice on identifying simple, compound, and complex sentences. In your Learn Portal dashboard, complete the assignment — twenty original sentences and ten run-on or fragment repairs with explanations. Book an advisor check-in if either pause felt shaky. Next up — Module Four, Vocabulary in Context.
